text: Hi, today we are going to look at the concept of development of mesopancreas which actually helps in safe and complete dissection of the uncinate process from the retroperitoneal structures and the superior mesentric artery and the superior mesentric vein. After the dissection of the superior mesentric vein, it is very important to understand the origin of or the insertion of the first regional vein as shown by the yellow arrow.

Following the tunneling and dissection of the superior mesenteric vein, the surgeon performs lateral dissection of the portal vein, maintaining proximity to the superior mesenteric vein rather than the pancreas.
The surgeon applies constant traction to the duodenum in the supracolic region, performing the Cattell-Braasch maneuver to fully expose the superior mesenteric vein.

The assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein and mesentery to the left, while the surgeon retracts the duodenum to the right in the supracolic compartment. The dissection focuses on removing the entire mesopancreas.

The surgeon identifies the origin of the first jejunal vein, highlighted by a yellow arrow, as it inserts into the superior mesenteric vein. This vein collects blood from the jejunal loop and passes behind the superior mesenteric artery before insertion.

The assistant surgeon retracts the superior mesenteric vein to the left side, exposing the first jejunal branch. Simultaneously, the surgeon retracts the duodenum and jejunal loop in the supracolic compartment to the left side, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon retracts the tissue to expose the first jejunal vein, marked by a yellow arrow, located above the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses suction dissection over the superior mesenteric artery, aiming to reposition it to the right of the superior mesenteric vein, bringing the first jejunal branch anterior to the artery.

The first jejunal vein is visible as it reinserts into the superior mesenteric vein. Behind the first jejunal vein, the superior mesenteric artery is observable, showing pulsations. The surgeon ligates and dissects the tributaries of the first jejunal vein without sacrificing the vein itself.

The pulsations of the superior mesenteric artery are visible just behind the first jejunal vein branch. The first jejunal vein is observed kinking and rotating above the superior mesenteric artery, then curving down behind it, hooking around the artery.

The surgeon identifies the first jejunal vein and continues dissection right underneath it, above the superior mesenteric artery. The dissection proceeds above the artery to ensure complete removal of the mesopancreas.

The surgeon retracts the specimen to the left side of the screen while the assistant retracts the superior mesenteric vein or portal vein to the right, exposing the mesopancreas or pancreatic mesentery, which is clearly visible over the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon and assistant apply traction to expose the superior mesenteric artery. The first jejunal vein branch is visible, with tributaries being clipped. The first regional branch is seen hooking around the superior mesenteric artery, facilitating the complete removal of the mesopancreas.

The surgical team has reached the retroperitoneum, with the pancreatic parenchyma fully dissected and included in the specimen. The superior mesenteric artery is visible, positioned to the right of the superior mesenteric vein due to opposing tractions applied by the surgeon and assistant.

The surgeon uses a harmonic device to dissect along the superior mesenteric artery, which is visible behind the first jejunal vein. This technique elongates the mesopancreas and facilitates the complete resection of the uncinate process.

The surgeon continues dissection along the superior mesenteric artery, encountering the superior pancreaticoduodenal vein as it enters the superior mesenteric vein. This step is crucial for the complete removal of the mesopancreas.
